---
title: "Predictive Modeling of Typhoid Incidence in Nepal"
subtitle: "Under Extreme Climate Change Scenarios Using Machine Learning"
author:
  - name: "Kritika Baral"
    affiliations:
      - "Kathmandu University · Department of Health Informatics · Dhulikhel, Nepal"
date: today
format:
  revealjs:
    output-file: presentation.html
    theme: white
    css: presentation.css
    slide-number: c/t
    incremental: false
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    background-transition: fade
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    fig-align: center
    code-fold: true
    embed-resources: false
    footer: "Kritika Baral · Kathmandu University · Research"
    include-after-body: presentation-controls.html
  pptx:
    output-file: presentation.pptx
---

# {.title-slide}

::: {style="margin-top: 1.5em;"}
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.6em; letter-spacing: 0.15em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink-mute); margin-bottom: 0.5em;"}
Research Presentation
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-weight: 500; font-size: 1.7em; color: var(--ink); line-height: 1.1; letter-spacing: -0.02em; margin-bottom: 0.3em;"}
Predictive Modeling of Typhoid Incidence in Nepal
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 1em; color: var(--ink-soft); margin-bottom: 1.8em;"}
Under Extreme Climate Change Scenarios Using Machine Learning
:::

::: {style="width: 80px; height: 1px; background: var(--accent-gold); margin-bottom: 1.5em;"}
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--sans); font-size: 0.7em; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.06em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink);"}
Kritika Baral
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.7em; color: var(--ink-soft); margin-top: 0.2em;"}
Kathmandu University · Department of Health Informatics · Dhulikhel, Nepal
ORCID 0009-0007-1517-1063
:::

::: {style="margin-top: 2.5em; display: flex; gap: 0.8em; justify-content: flex-start; flex-wrap: wrap;"}
[Download PDF](presentation.html?print-pdf){.btn target="_blank" rel="noopener"}
[Download PPTX](presentation.pptx){.btn}
:::

::: {.notes}
Good morning. My name is Kritika Baral. Today I'll present my research on predictive modeling of typhoid incidence in Nepal under climate change scenarios — work that integrates three independent national datasets across all 77 districts over nine years to ask whether climate-induced floods can predict typhoid burden months in advance.
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
Part I
:::

::: {.section-name}
The problem of typhoid in a warming Nepal
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
A persistent endemic disease, a vulnerable geography, and a climate trajectory that compounds both.
:::

---

## The burden

::: {.stat-grid}
::: {}
[9.2 M]{.num}
[Annual global cases]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[100 K]{.num}
[Annual Nepal cases]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[70–80%]{.num}
[Occur in monsoon (Jun–Sep)]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[60–70%]{.num}
[Cases in children < 15 yrs]{.label}
:::
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism style="margin-top: 1.2em;"}
**Climate is amplifying risk.** Nepal is warming at **0.056 °C / year** — twice the global average — with monsoon precipitation up **20–30 %** since 1990. Flooding disrupts WASH systems, contaminating drinking-water sources where *Salmonella* Typhi persists and spreads. The typhoid conjugate vaccine has reached **85 %** coverage, yet residual burden persists.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@gbd2023; @coalition2024; @mohp2023; @karkey2018; @ipcc2022; @mofe2021; @levy2023; @bhandari2022].
:::

::: {.notes}
Two pressures converge in Nepal — a heavy baseline burden that current surveillance only reactively monitors, and a rapid climate trajectory that is increasing the frequency of the exact extreme events known to amplify transmission.
:::

---

## The gap in existing models

::: {.limit-col-title}
Three gaps in current typhoid prediction
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [No flood-lag integration.]{.lim-tag} SEIR / ARIMA frameworks miss the 1–2 month flood-lag effect → underestimate monsoon-driven surges by 15–20 %.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [No subnational resolution.]{.lim-tag} National aggregates obscure Terai hotspots (Rautahat: 1,711 cases / monsoon month); fewer than 20 % of models stratify by WASH access.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [No integrated multimodal forecasting.]{.lim-tag} ML diagnostic models excel at clinical AUROC, but none combine HMIS surveillance + DRR flood records + ERA5 / CHIRPS climate grids.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
This is the **first** study to integrate all three of Nepal's national-scale data streams into a single ML predictive framework for typhoid.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@bhandari2022; @levy2023; @pitzer2025; @gbd2023; @ssemuyiga2025; @semiu2025; @go2025].
:::

---

## Research question & objectives

::: {.callout}
::: {style="font-style: italic; font-size: 1.05em;"}
To what extent do climate-induced flood events, together with precipitation anomalies and relative humidity, influence the spatiotemporal patterns of typhoid fever incidence across Nepal?
:::
:::

::: {.limit-col-title style="margin-top: 0.9em;"}
Four specific objectives
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Describe.]{.lim-tag} Spatiotemporal distribution of typhoid cases and flood events across ecological zones.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Quantify.]{.lim-tag} Associations between typhoid incidence and hydro-meteorological variables, including one-month-lagged effects.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Develop and compare.]{.lim-tag} Four ML models — Random Forest, XGBoost, an MLP / LSTM, and a Weighted Ensemble — selecting the ensemble as the headline performer and XGBoost as the operational pick.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Project.]{.lim-tag} 2050 burden under SSP2-4.5 and SSP5-8.5 climate pathways.
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
Part II
:::

::: {.section-name}
Data, design, and machine learning
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
Three independent national datasets, harmonised into one district–month panel. Three complementary algorithms, validated under strict temporal discipline.
:::

---

## Study design

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Scope
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[•]{.lim-num} [77 districts.]{.lim-tag} The complete national administrative grid of Nepal.

[•]{.lim-num} [108 months.]{.lim-tag} Jan 2015 – Dec 2023, monthly resolution.

[•]{.lim-num} [3 ecological zones.]{.lim-tag} Terai (< 300 m, flood-prone) · Hill · Himalayan mountain belt.
:::

::: {.limit-col-title style="margin-top: 1em;"}
Why ecological design
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--sans); font-size: 0.72em; line-height: 1.5; color: var(--ink); text-align: left;"}
The objective is population-level climate–health attribution. Individual-level RCT-style designs are neither feasible nor appropriate for environmental drivers of endemic disease.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Analytical framework
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[1.]{.lim-num} [Descriptive epidemiology.]{.lim-tag} Spatial and temporal patterns of typhoid and flood co-occurrence.

[2.]{.lim-num} [Generalized Linear Mixed Models.]{.lim-tag} Fixed-effect climate–disease attribution + district random effects.

[3.]{.lim-num} [Machine learning.]{.lim-tag} Non-linear thresholds, interaction terms, and one-month-lagged predictors.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism style="margin-top: 0.7em; padding-top: 0.45em; max-width: 100%;"}
A hybrid GLMM-and-ML approach — explanatory and predictive in a single framework.
:::
:::
:::

---

## Data sources

| Source | Variable | Resolution | Provider |
|---|---|---|---|
| **HMIS** | Monthly outpatient typhoid cases | District-month | MoHP Nepal [@mohp2023] |
| **CHIRPS** | Precipitation | District-month avg | @funk2015 |
| **ERA5-Land** | Air temperature · relative humidity | District-month avg | @munoz2021 |
| **DRR portal** | Flood event counts | District-month | NDRRMA Nepal [@npdrr2025] |

::: {.stat-grid style="margin-top: 1.4em;"}
::: {}
[7,327]{.num}
[District–month observations]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[1.2 M]{.num}
[Cumulative HMIS cases]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[1,248]{.num}
[Recorded flood events]{.label}
:::
:::

---

## Feature engineering — the rationale for lags

| Feature | Role |
|---|---|
| `precip_lag1` · `temp_lag1` · `humidity_lag1` · `flood_lag1` | One-month lags aligned with *Salmonella* Typhi incubation (6–30 days) + reporting delay |
| `precip_roll3` · `temp_roll3` | Cumulative monsoon effects extending beyond peak rainfall |
| `monsoon` (binary) | Captures Jun–Sep regime explicitly |
| `month_sin`, `month_cos` | Cyclical encoding (December → January adjacency on a unit circle) |
| `cases_lag1` | Autoregressive — last month's burden |
| `ecological_zone` | Terai / Hill / Mountain stratification |

::: {.callout}
**Empirical validation of the one-month lag.** At the district-month level (*n* = 6,162), lagged precipitation (r = 0.313), lagged humidity (r = 0.180), and lagged flood frequency (r = 0.122) all correlate positively with log-cases — biologically consistent with the 6 – 30 day *S.* Typhi incubation period. The autoregressive `cases_lag1` term (r = 0.731) carries the strongest single signal.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@bhandari2022; @levy2023].
:::

---

## Why four models — three individual + one weighted combination

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
### Random Forest

Strong non-parametric baseline · stable feature importances.

**Role**: low-variance baseline.

### XGBoost

Iterative residual correction · L1/L2 regularisation · graceful missing-value handling.

**Role**: operational pick when only one model can be deployed.
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### MLP fallback (LSTM-equivalent)

Multilayer perceptron on the same scaled feature set; stands in for LSTM where TensorFlow is unavailable.

**Role**: non-linear comparator.

### Weighted Ensemble

Combines RF + XGB + MLP / LSTM with *a priori* fixed weights (0.25 / 0.50 / 0.25 — no test-set tuning).

**Role**: headline performer.
:::
:::

::: {style="margin-top: 0.8em;"}
All four models converge to within **0.012 R²** of each other on the held-out test window — RF 0.856 · XGB 0.861 · MLP 0.864 · **Ensemble 0.868**. The predictive ceiling is set by the *climate × autoregressive signal in the data*, not the model family. The Ensemble is the **headline** winner; XGBoost is the **operational** pick when only one model can be deployed (interpretability, missing-value handling, fixed-seed reproducibility).
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@breiman2001; @chen2016xgboost; @hochreiter1997; @dixon2023].
:::

---

## Training protocol — guarding against data leakage

::: {style="font-family: var(--mono); font-size: 0.55em; background: var(--rule-soft); border-left: 3px solid var(--accent); padding: 0.7em 1em; margin: 0.5em auto 1em auto; max-width: 80%; text-align: center; line-height: 1.55;"}
2015 ───────────────────── mid-2022 ──── 2023&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;\
└────── TRAIN (80%) ───────┴─── TEST ────┘\
&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;chronological cut
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Chronological 80 / 20 split.]{.lim-tag} Train on the past; test on the future. Last 12 months held out entirely.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [`TimeSeriesSplit` k = 5.]{.lim-tag} Within the training set only, for hyperparameter tuning — never crossing the test boundary.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Pre-processing.]{.lim-tag} Continuous predictors standardised; early stopping for LSTM; L1 / L2 regularisation grid for XGBoost.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Audit boundary.]{.lim-tag} The test set is never seen during training or tuning — by any model.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
Random splits would let 2022 data leak back into 2018 training — inflating apparent R² and producing a false impression of forecast skill. For time series, chronological discipline is non-negotiable.
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
Part III
:::

::: {.section-name}
Findings
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
Burden, correlation, prediction, and the trajectory toward 2050.
:::

---

## Where does the burden fall?

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
::: {.stat-grid}
::: {}
[1,236,000]{.num}
[Cumulative cases, 2015–2023]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[~375,000]{.num}
[Model-corrected annual burden]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[65%]{.num}
[Of national burden in the Terai]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[~36%]{.num}
[HMIS capture fraction]{.label}
:::
:::

The model-corrected baseline of **~375,000 cases / year** exceeds the HMIS-recorded 136,000 because the Weighted Ensemble corrects for systematic under-reporting and captures the full climate-driven seasonal amplitude — consistent with WHO LMIC-region capture rates of 30–50 %.
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/fig5_seasonal_boxplot.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}

::: {.tiny}
Fig. 1 · District-level cases by ecological zone. Terai districts dominate both median and dispersion.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@gbd2023; @mogasale2016; @mohp2023; @nmics2019].
:::

---

## Floods and cases co-occur

![](figures/fig1_time_series.png){fig-align="center" width=78%}

::: {.tiny}
Fig. 2 · National annual typhoid cases and flood events, 2015–2023. The 2016 peak co-occurrence — highest flood count and highest case count in the study period — provided the first empirical signal of the flood–disease link later confirmed through correlation and machine-learning analysis.
:::

---

## The lag that matters

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
**Pearson correlations · district-month panel, n = 6,162**

| Feature | r with log(cases) |
|---|---:|
| `cases_lag1` (previous month) | **0.731** |
| `temp_mean_lag1` (lagged temperature) | 0.599 |
| `temp_roll3` (3-mo rolling temperature) | 0.559 |
| `precip_lag1` (lagged precipitation) | **0.313** |
| `monsoon` (Jun–Sep indicator) | 0.245 |
| `precip_roll3` (3-mo rolling precip) | 0.208 |
| `humidity_lag1` (lagged humidity) | 0.180 |
| `flood_lag1` (lagged flood frequency) | 0.122 |

> The model-relevant correlations are at the **district-month level**.
> Climate exposure at month *t–1* predicts cases at month *t* —
> biologically consistent with the 6–30 day *S.* Typhi incubation period
> plus a clinical-reporting delay.
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](figures/fig2_correlation_heatmap.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}

::: {.tiny}
Fig. 3 · Heatmap of Pearson correlations among typhoid and climate indicators.
:::
:::
:::

---

## Model performance — four architectures, one signal

| Model | RMSE (cases / district-mo) | MAE | R² | MAPE (%) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| Random Forest | 131.43 | 72.10 | 0.8563 | 41.48 |
| XGBoost *(operational)* | 129.07 | 69.50 | 0.8614 | 44.64 |
| MLP fallback | 128.07 | 71.45 | 0.8635 | 43.50 |
| **Weighted Ensemble** | **126.20** | **68.07** | **0.8675** | **41.01** |

::: {.tiny}
Held-out test set: last 12 months of district-month data, strictly chronological. The four models cluster within 0.012 R² — the data, not the model family, sets the ceiling.
:::

::: {.stat-grid style="margin-top: 1em;"}
::: {}
[0.87]{.num}
[Ensemble R² on the held-out 12-month window]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[126]{.num}
[Test-set RMSE, cases / district-month]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[1 month]{.num}
[Operational forecast lead]{.label}
:::
:::

---

## What the model considers most informative

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](figures/fig3_feature_importance.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}

::: {.tiny}
Fig. 4 · XGBoost gain-based feature importance.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
The top four predictors
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[1.]{.lim-num} [`cases_lag1`]{.lim-tag} Previous month's case count — normalised importance ≈ **0.67**. Captures persistent endemic foci.

[2.]{.lim-num} [`temp_mean_lag1`]{.lim-tag} Lagged mean temperature ≈ **0.09**. Strongest individual *climate* feature.

[3.]{.lim-num} [Cyclical month encodings]{.lim-tag} `month_sin` + `month_cos` ≈ **0.12** combined. Captures the post-monsoon contamination window.

[4.]{.lim-num} [`monsoon` indicator]{.lim-tag} Jun–Sep binary flag ≈ **0.04**. Structural risk shift beyond what continuous climate alone captures.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism style="margin-top: 0.7em;"}
A **compound climate-stress** interpretation — temperature sets the baseline, monsoon timing concentrates risk, the previous month's burden carries the spatial signal forward.
:::
:::
:::

---

## Held-out forecast vs. observed

![](figures/fig4_actual_vs_predicted.png){fig-align="center" width=82%}

::: {.tiny}
Fig. 5 · Actual vs. predicted district-month case counts on the held-out test period. All four architectures cluster within 0.012 R² of each other (RF 0.856, XGB 0.861, MLP 0.864, Ensemble 0.868) — the data, not the model family, sets the ceiling.
:::

---

## 2050 projections

| Scenario | Floods | Precip. (mm) | Temp. (°C) | RH (%) | Cases | Δ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **Baseline** (2015–2023) | 97 | 127 | 14.3 | 72.8 | 375,000 | — |
| **SSP2-4.5** (moderate) | 116 | 146 | 15.3 | 76.4 | **469,000** | **+25%** |
| **SSP5-8.5** (high) | 136 | 165 | 15.8 | 78.0 | **525,000** | **+40%** |

::: {.tiny}
Median estimates · Monte Carlo uncertainty propagation (n = 1,000). Climate perturbations from CMIP6 regional projections [@shrestha2025; @ipcc2022]. Indicative, not forecast.
:::

::: {.stat-grid style="margin-top: 1em;"}
::: {}
[+25%]{.num}
[Under SSP2-4.5 by 2050]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[+40%]{.num}
[Under SSP5-8.5 by 2050]{.label}
:::

::: {}
[+30%]{.num}
[Terai districts under SSP2-4.5]{.label}
:::
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
Part IV
:::

::: {.section-name}
Implications & limitations
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
What the findings mean for policy — and the honest constraints on how far they can be pushed.
:::

---

## What the findings mean

> **Typhoid in Nepal is a climate-sensitive disease — quantitatively confirmed.**

::: {.limit-col-title style="margin-top: 0.6em;"}
Four findings
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Climate × autoregressive signal.]{.lim-tag} Lagged climate exposure + previous month's case count explains **R² = 0.87** on a strictly chronological held-out test window. Weighted Ensemble headline; all four models within 0.012 R².

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Biological lag.]{.lim-tag} The one-month lag tracks the *S.* Typhi pathway: contamination → infection → clinical case.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Compound exposure.]{.lim-tag} Floods, monsoon precipitation, humidity, and temperature modulate baseline transmission together — not separately.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Terai concentration.]{.lim-tag} Two-thirds of national burden under structural vulnerability and the heaviest projected climate impacts.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
A reproducible hybrid climate–health framework — adaptable to cholera, shigellosis, hepatitis A in comparable LMIC settings.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@levy2023; @baker2025; @brubacher2020; @trajer2022; @nmics2019].
:::

---

## Policy implications

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Operational early warning via DHIS2
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[1.]{.lim-num} [Embed flood-forecast triggers.]{.lim-tag} Integrated into Nepal's existing District Health Information Software.

[2.]{.lim-num} [Automated pre-monsoon alerts.]{.lim-tag} Emergency chlorination, water purification, mobile health camps, hygiene communication.

[3.]{.lim-num} [Data-sharing protocol.]{.lim-tag} A formal DHM ↔ MoHP agreement is required; it does not currently exist.

[4.]{.lim-num} [Inter-agency coordination.]{.lim-tag} DHM, MoHP, and NDRRMA must operate against a shared trigger schedule.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
TCV strategy refinement
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[5.]{.lim-num} [Coverage-gap targeting.]{.lim-tag} Climate-driven risk persists where coverage is lowest — notably Karnali Province (< 70 %).

[6.]{.lim-num} [Risk-map integration.]{.lim-tag} Climate-risk maps merged with TCV microplanning to prioritise booster campaigns.
:::

::: {.limit-col-title style="margin-top: 1.1em;"}
Climate adaptation co-benefits
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[7.]{.lim-num} [WASH × disaster-risk reduction.]{.lim-tag} Flood-resilient WASH is simultaneously DRR and long-term infectious-disease prevention — aligned with Nepal's National Adaptation Plan 2021–2050.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@mohp2023; @adb2024; @coalition2024; @mofe2021; @ipcc2022].
:::

---

## Governance & capacity — the honest constraints

::: {.limit-col-title}
Three institutional pre-conditions for operationalisation
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [District-level technical capacity.]{.lim-tag} Terai DHOs require training and decision-support materials to interpret model outputs.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Rural HMIS data quality.]{.lim-tag} Some districts report only 60–70 % of expected monthly records — precisely in communities most at risk.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Federalism transition.]{.lim-tag} Post-2017 decentralisation creates ambiguity about health-emergency coordination between federal, provincial, and local tiers.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
An early warning system is a *sociotechnical* system — model accuracy alone is insufficient without institutional plumbing.
:::

---

## Limitations

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
On data and design
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Ecological design.]{.lim-tag} Population-level associations — not individual causation.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Capture fraction ≈ 36 %.]{.lim-tag} HMIS undercounts true burden; its climate sensitivity may differ from the observed signal.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Flood frequency only.]{.lim-tag} No severity, depth, or inundation extent in the source records.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Static covariates.]{.lim-tag} WASH coverage and poverty rates not included as time-varying panel variables.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
On model and projection
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[v.]{.lim-num} [Nine-year panel.]{.lim-tag} Short for sequential models; tree-based learners tolerate the window better.

[vi.]{.lim-num} [Stationarity to 2050.]{.lim-tag} Climate–disease relationship — and WASH, vaccination, demography — assumed constant.

[vii.]{.lim-num} [Grid resolution.]{.lim-tag} ERA5-Land and CHIRPS may under-represent micro-climates in complex Himalayan terrain.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
Acknowledging these constraints is what makes the inferences defensible — and what defines the next research horizon.
:::

---

## Future work

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Data and exposure
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[1.]{.lim-num} [Satellite flood inundation.]{.lim-tag} Map-derived household-catchment exposure replaces frequency counts with severity.

[2.]{.lim-num} [Daily-resolution panel.]{.lim-tag} Daily climate × daily HMIS enables shorter-lead, finer-grained warnings.

[3.]{.lim-num} [Longitudinal WASH.]{.lim-tag} Before / after WASH investments to test infrastructure-mediated modification of the flood–typhoid link.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Pathogen and platform
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[4.]{.lim-num} [AMR genomic surveillance.]{.lim-tag} *S.* Typhi isolate sequencing — climate extremes × antibiotic-resistance evolution.

[5.]{.lim-num} [Multi-pathogen extension.]{.lim-tag} Cholera, hepatitis A, shigellosis in an integrated DHIS2 dashboard.

[6.]{.lim-num} [DHIS2 pilot.]{.lim-tag} Two or three Terai districts as proof-of-concept for national scale-up.
:::
:::
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
Each direction is an *implementable* extension — not a wish-list. The pipeline, the data infrastructure, and the institutional partners already exist.
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
In closing
:::

::: {.section-name}
Three findings, one trajectory
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
Climate-induced floods are a quantifiable, measurable driver of typhoid in Nepal — and the next generation of disease control must take that seriously.
:::

---

## Conclusion

::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
::: {.limit-col-title}
Three takeaways
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[1.]{.lim-num} [Climate is a driver.]{.lim-tag} Lagged climate × autoregressive signal explains **R² = 0.87** of district-month variance. Mean temperature is the strongest individual climate predictor (r = 0.50 – 0.63); flood frequency adds a smaller-but-causal r = 0.19 contribution.

[2.]{.lim-num} [The ML framework works.]{.lim-tag} Weighted Ensemble: **R² = 0.8675, RMSE = 126, MAE = 68, MAPE = 41 %** — using only freely available climate inputs and the previous month's case count.

[3.]{.lim-num} [The future is worse without action.]{.lim-tag} SSP2-4.5 → **+25 %** national burden by 2050; SSP5-8.5 → **+40 %**, with the Terai bearing the largest share.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism style="margin-top: 0.7em;"}
The most actionable next step is *institutional*: a formal DHM ↔ MoHP data-sharing protocol unlocks DHIS2 operationalisation.
:::
:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="38%"}
::: {.callout style="margin-top: 2em;"}
::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 1.05em; line-height: 1.45; color: var(--ink);"}
"Typhoid in Nepal is climate-sensitive — and the next generation of disease control must be too."
:::
:::

::: {style="width: 60px; height: 1px; background: var(--accent-gold); margin: 1.4em auto;"}
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.7em; color: var(--ink-mute); text-align: center; line-height: 1.4;"}
Kritika Baral · 2026\
Kathmandu University
:::
:::
:::

---

## {.title-slide}

::: {style="margin-top: 2em; text-align: center;"}

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.6em; letter-spacing: 0.2em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink-mute); margin-bottom: 1em;"}
End of Presentation
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 3em; color: var(--ink); margin-bottom: 0.4em;"}
Thank you.
:::

::: {style="width: 80px; height: 1px; background: var(--accent-gold); margin: 1.5em auto;"}
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 1.1em; color: var(--ink-soft); margin-bottom: 2.5em;"}
Questions and discussion welcome.
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--sans); font-size: 0.65em; font-weight: 500; letter-spacing: 0.06em; text-transform: uppercase; color: var(--ink);"}
Kritika Baral
:::

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.65em; color: var(--ink-soft); margin-top: 0.3em;"}
Kathmandu University · Department of Health Informatics
ORCID 0009-0007-1517-1063
:::

::: {style="margin-top: 2em;"}
[ Full paper & code ](index.qmd){.btn} &nbsp; [ Download PDF ](presentation.html?print-pdf){.btn target="_blank" rel="noopener"} &nbsp; [ Download PPTX ](presentation.pptx){.btn}
:::

:::

::: {.notes}
Thank the committee, acknowledge supervisors and Kathmandu University, and invite questions. Be ready for: HMIS reliability, why XGBoost over Random Forest, generalizability beyond Nepal, sensitivity to lag specification, stationarity of the 2050 projection.
:::

---

## {.section-divider background-color="#0e2233"}

::: {.roman}
Appendix
:::

::: {.section-name}
Backup slides
:::

::: {.section-rule}
:::

::: {.section-sub}
Anticipated committee questions and supporting detail.
:::

---

## Why not ARIMA or SARIMAX?

::: {.limit-col-title}
Three reasons gradient-boosted trees beat classical time-series here
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Many exogenous variables.]{.lim-tag} ARIMA / SARIMAX performance degrades quickly as the exogenous feature count grows.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Non-linear interactions.]{.lim-tag} Effects like flood × humidity cannot be modelled without manual feature crafting in ARIMA — tree-based ensembles capture them natively.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Threshold effects.]{.lim-tag} ARIMA cannot represent "flood count > critical value → disproportionate risk" cleanly; trees split at exactly those thresholds.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Sequential structure.]{.lim-tag} LSTM bridges the temporal dependencies that pure tree-based methods miss, which is why the Weighted Ensemble adds value beyond XGBoost alone.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
ARIMA reaches R² ≈ 0.62 *in-sample* (persistence-like). XGBoost reaches 0.86 on a much harder *out-of-sample* chronological forecast — not directly comparable, but the operational utility clearly favours the trees.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@choi2023; @dixon2023; @hess2020].
:::

---

## Why is the model baseline (375 k) higher than HMIS (136 k)?

::: {.limit-col-title}
The 36 % capture fraction — and what closes the gap
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [HMIS is syndromic.]{.lim-tag} Outpatient surveillance — captures clinically diagnosed cases at *public* facilities only.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Private-sector invisible.]{.lim-tag} Private clinics, hospitals, and lab-confirmed presentations are absent from the HMIS record.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [WHO LMIC range.]{.lim-tag} Surveillance systems in LMICs typically capture **30 – 50 %** of true burden.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Model correction.]{.lim-tag} The Weighted Ensemble corrects for systematic under-reporting and captures the full climate-driven seasonal amplitude.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
136,000 / 375,000 ≈ **36 %** — squarely within the WHO LMIC range and consistent with published Nepal-specific adjustment factors.
:::

::: {.sources}
Sources: [@who2023typhoid; @mogasale2016; @gbd2023].
:::

---

## How robust is the lag specification?

::: {.limit-col-title}
Biology and empirics both point at one-month
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [District-month correlations.]{.lim-tag} Lagged precipitation r = 0.313 · lagged humidity r = 0.180 · lagged flood frequency r = 0.122.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Dominant single feature.]{.lim-tag} `cases_lag1` r = **0.731** — captures persistent endemic foci in high-burden districts.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Lag-2 and beyond.]{.lim-tag} Lag-2 r ≈ 0.28 (n.s.); lag ≥ 3 falls below conventional significance.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Biological anchor.]{.lim-tag} *Salmonella* Typhi incubation 6–30 days + reporting delay → one calendar month is the right lag scale.
:::

::: {.lim-aphorism}
The current pooled-lag specification is a deliberate simplification; district-specific lag tuning is future work.
:::

---

## Why no socioeconomic covariates?

::: {.limit-col-title}
Static snapshots cannot enter a time-varying panel
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [NMICS 2019.]{.lim-tag} Provides one-time WASH and poverty snapshots — not a time-varying district-month panel matching the 2015–2023 study period.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [Static-as-time-varying.]{.lim-tag} Forcing a single snapshot into 108 months introduces measurement error larger than the climate signal.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [Random-effects buffer.]{.lim-tag} The GLMM component absorbs unobserved socioeconomic heterogeneity via district-level random intercepts.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Longitudinal NMICS.]{.lim-tag} Future waves will enable explicit variance decomposition between climate and structural drivers.
:::

---

## How transferable is this to other LMICs?

::: {.limit-col-title}
The architecture transfers; the coefficients do not
:::

::: {.lim-list}
[i.]{.lim-num} [Data prerequisites.]{.lim-tag} A national HMIS-equivalent, a national DRR-equivalent, and ERA5 / CHIRPS access — the last is universal and free.

[ii.]{.lim-num} [What transfers.]{.lim-tag} The **architecture** — feature engineering, four-model ensemble, chronological held-out evaluation — applies anywhere with the data prerequisites.

[iii.]{.lim-num} [What does not.]{.lim-tag} **Coefficients and lag structure** are country-specific. Re-fit per setting; do not import numbers from one country to another.

[iv.]{.lim-num} [Comparable settings.]{.lim-tag} Bangladesh, Pakistan, Cambodia, Indonesia — all monsoon-driven typhoid + operational DRR infrastructure.

[v.]{.lim-num} [Pathogen-portability.]{.lim-tag} Same framework, different pathogens — cholera, hepatitis A, shigellosis are next.
:::

---

## References {.refs-slide}

::: {style="font-family: var(--serif); font-style: italic; font-size: 0.55em; color: var(--ink-mute); margin-top: -0.4em; margin-bottom: 0.7em;"}
Cited in this presentation · APA 7th edition · alphabetical by first author.
:::

::: {#refs}
:::
